data.title: Intel outlines Q2 revenue $13.8B-$14.8B and non-GAAP EPS $0.20 as CPU demand outpaces supply
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Intel (INTC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Lip-Bu Tan framed Q1 as a supply-constrained upcycle, saying, "Revenue, gross margin, and earnings per share were all above the high end of guidance, marking our sixth consecutive quarter of exceeding financial expectations," while adding, "demand continues to run ahead of supply for all our businesses, especially for Xeon server CPUs."
  • Tan emphasized manufacturing ramps and AI-driven CPU positioning, stating, "Intel 3-based Xeon 6 and Intel 18A based Core Series 3 products are now in full volume production ramp," and argued that "the CPU is reinserting itself as the indispensable foundation of the AI era" as customers rebalance CPU-to-accelerator configurations.
  • Tan highlighted multiple partnerships, including, "Our recent announcement with SambaNova Systems," and said, "We recently announced our partnership with SpaceX, xAI and Tesla to support Terafab," describing work to "explore innovative ways to refactor silicon process technology."
  • CFO David Zinsner attributed Q1 outperformance to supply and mix, saying, "We delivered robust Q1 results, reflecting strong demand and better-than-expected available supply," and reported, "First quarter revenue was $13.6 billion."
  • Zinsner detailed profitability and cash flow, stating, "Non-GAAP gross margin came in at 41%," "non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.29," and "Q1 operating cash flow was $1.1 billion" with "gross CapEx of $5 billion" and "adjusted free cash flow of minus $2 billion."

Outlook

  • Zinsner guided Q2 revenue to "a range of $13.8 billion to $14.8 billion" and said, "At the midpoint of $14.3 billion, we forecast a gross margin of 39%, a tax rate of 11% and EPS of $0.20, all on a non-GAAP basis."
  • He cautioned on macro and input costs, noting "constraints and rising prices around key components like memory, wafers and substrates," and said Intel is "prudently planning for PC demand to weaken in the second half of the year" with "the full year PC unit TAM to be down low double-digit percent."
  • Against that, Zinsner tightened commentary on servers, stating, "Our outlook for server CPU demand has improved over the last 90 days," and projecting "a strong year of double-digit unit growth for the industry and for us with momentum extending into 2027."
  • Compared with Q4 2025, the tone shifted from "our internal supply constraints are most acute in Q1" (prior call) to Q1 2026 guidance built around sequential growth in both CCG and DCAI on improving supply, while also calling out a Q2 gross margin step-down driven by early-node mix.

Financial Results

  • Zinsner said Q1 results benefited from pricing and inventory actions: "We also benefited from improved product mix and pricing actions," and "Non-GAAP gross margin came in at 41%," including "previously reserved inventory" and "better yields on Intel 18A" offsetting early-ramp costs.
  • Segment disclosures centered on supply and AI mix: CCG revenue was "$7.7 billion, down 6% sequentially," with "AI PC revenue grew 8% sequentially and now represents greater than 60% of our client CPU mix," while DCAI revenue was "$5.1 billion, an increase of 7% sequentially and 22% year-over-year."
  • Intel Foundry remained a major drag as revenue rose on EUV mix but losses persisted: "Intel Foundry delivered revenue of $5.4 billion, up 20% sequentially," "External foundry revenue was $174 million," and "Intel Foundry operating loss in Q1 was $2.4 billion," which Zinsner said should improve as "18A continues to ramp into volume and yields improve further."
  • Zinsner updated capital and balance sheet positioning, saying 2026 capex is now "flat to last year versus our prior expectation of flat to down," and described a Fab 34 Ireland buyout: "we funded our purchase with approximately $7.7 billion in cash and $6.5 billion in new debt."

Q&A

  • Benjamin Reitzes, Melius Research LLC: asked about long-term agreements and visibility; CEO Tan replied, "Google is one of the multi- long-term contract agreement in Q1," and CFO Zinsner added, "most of these agreements are structured with volume and pricing" and "usually somewhere between 3 and 5 years."
  • Ross Seymore, Deutsche Bank AG: pressed on server CPU competitiveness and whether higher capex is needed to scale supply; Tan said the CPU-to-GPU ratio is shifting as "it used to be 1 and 8, and now it's 1:4," while emphasizing yield and cycle-time work and customer decisions tied to PDK maturity.
  • Stacy Rasgon, Bernstein: challenged the Q2 margin setup; Zinsner cited "Panther Lake volume increases" and said 18A is "a pretty decent headwind" as volumes rise, while also flagging "memory going up" and "substrates are going up" as second-half offsets.
  • Timothy Arcuri, UBS: asked how much demand is being missed; Zinsner declined to quantify but said, "Let's just say it starts with a B. So it's meaningful," and Tan stayed non-specific on Terafab structure: "we would update you as we go."
  • Christopher Muse, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked about output levers and advanced packaging scale; Zinsner said Intel is "increasing wafer starts" across nodes and described packaging demand as "in the billions of dollars per year kind of level."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to skeptical in spots, with Rasgon saying, "I'm just a little confused" on gross margin drivers and repeatedly pressing on PC demand shape.
  • Management tone was positive but guarded on constraints and costs, with Zinsner repeatedly emphasizing supply limits and input inflation, and Tan using confidence language such as "I'm confident" and "stay tuned" while avoiding specifics on unannounced contracts.
  • Versus Q4 2025, management messaging moved from a Q1 trough narrative (prior quarter) to stronger confidence, including Tan’s line, "A year ago, the conversation about Intel was about whether we could survive. Today, it's about how quickly we can add manufacturing capacity."

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 featured a sharper supply-and-demand imbalance narrative than Q4 2025, with Tan now stating, "demand continues to run ahead of supply for all our businesses," while Q4 emphasized Q1 being the most acute constraint period.
  • Guidance language improved sequentially from Q4’s Q1 outlook of "$11.7 billion to $12.7 billion" revenue and "breakeven EPS" to Q1’s Q2 guide that includes non-GAAP EPS of "$0.20," while explicitly warning that Q1 inventory benefits "aren't expected to repeat in Q2."
  • Foundry commentary evolved from customer-decision timing on 14A in the "second half of this year" (Q4) to Q1 specifics on PDK progress and expected "earlier design commitments" beginning in "the second half of 2026" and into "the first half of 2027."

Risks and Concerns

  • Zinsner warned that "macroeconomic and geopolitical environments are dynamic" and highlighted "rising prices" in memory and substrates as potential demand and margin headwinds.
  • Management repeatedly cited supply constraints as a limiter, with Zinsner saying Q1 revenue "would have been meaningfully higher" and later cautioning that certain Q1 inventory sales are "not sure we have that benefit" in Q2.
  • PC demand risk was called out directly: Intel is "prudently planning for PC demand to weaken in the second half of the year" even as near-term orders remain "very robust."

Final Takeaway

Tan and Zinsner portrayed Q1 as an execution-driven quarter with demand still ahead of supply, anchored by a rebound in server CPU momentum and a fast ramp of Intel 18A products that helped Q1 profitability but is expected to pressure Q2 gross margin as volumes rise. Management’s Q2 framework pointed to sequential growth across key segments alongside heightened caution on second-half PC demand and escalating input costs, while Q&A centered on long-term customer agreements, the magnitude of unmet demand, and whether yield and cycle-time improvements can translate into sustained supply expansion and improving foundry economics.

data.title: Associated Banc-Corp targets 17% to 19% 2026 period-end loan growth after American National close, with conversion slated for late Q3
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Associated Banc-Corp (ASB) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We entered 2026 with strong momentum" and the quarter reflected that, with CEO Andrew Harmening highlighting "annualized first quarter checking household growth of 2.2%" and "over $500 million of period end C&I loan growth" alongside "total deposits" up $179 million and "core customer deposits" up "over $800 million versus Q4." (President, CEO & Director Andrew Harmening)
  • Harmening emphasized accelerated expansion and integration execution, citing "launched our new C&I office in Dallas" and "launched a new national franchise banking vertical," and announcing American National Bank closed "on April 1" with conversion expected "late in the third quarter of this year." (President, CEO & Director Harmening)
  • CFO Derek Meyer tied near-term margin pressure to growth and funding mix, stating loan growth "outpaced the natural run rate of our deposit battery" and that the company "accelerated our funding to match, which put some short-term downward pressure on both NII and margin." (Executive VP & CFO Derek Meyer)
  • Credit commentary remained focused on monitoring macro uncertainty, with Chief Credit Officer Pat Ahern noting Moody’s February 2026 baseline includes "higher levels of inflation and tariff negotiations" and "less total rate cuts in the latter half of 2026 compared to prior quarter forecast." (Executive VP, Chief Credit Officer & Chicago Market President Patrick Ahern)

Outlook

  • Following the American National close, management said, "we haven't seen any major surprises, and our current expectations are largely in line with the assumptions provided when we announced the deal," while also noting the 2026 outlook "does not assume any material incremental growth expectations for the American National business in 2026." (President, CEO & Director Harmening)
  • For combined-company balance sheet growth expectations after including the acquisition, Harmening said the company now expects "2026 period-end loan growth of 17% to 19%" and "2026 period end total deposit growth of 17% to 19%, and period end customer deposit growth of 19% to 21%" versus stand-alone year-end 2025 results. (President, CEO & Director Harmening)
  • On margin impact from the acquisition, Harmening reiterated prior expectations and said the company had "initially forecasted a potential increase of 5 to 10 basis points" once purchase accounting marks are finalized. (President, CEO & Director Harmening)
  • Management signaled potential changes to legacy stand-alone net interest income expectations, with Meyer saying, "Our guidance was 5.5% to 6.5%" and, given balances and rate-cut assumptions, "our guidance would be more like 7% to 8%." (Executive VP & CFO Meyer)

Financial Results

  • The quarter’s earnings were stated as "$0.70 per share" and included "Q1 net interest income of $307 million" and "total noninterest income of $76 million," with Harmening adding that noninterest expense was "$219 million." (President, CEO & Director Harmening)
  • Balance sheet growth was led by commercial, with Harmening reporting "total loans grew by over $600 million" and "C&I balances growing $540 million" versus the prior quarter. (President, CEO & Director Harmening)
  • Profitability metrics reflected modest compression tied to funding acceleration, as Meyer said net interest income "decreased $3 million versus the prior quarter" and net interest margin "decreased 3 basis points to 3.03%." (Executive VP & CFO Meyer)
  • Capital and book-value metrics were framed as stable despite strong growth, with Meyer stating CET1 ended at "10.47%" and tangible book value per share finished at "$22.23." (Executive VP & CFO Meyer)
  • Credit costs remained low in management’s framing, as Ahern reported "a modest provision of $11 million" and "$5 million in net charge-offs" with "just 7 basis points" of net charge-offs for the quarter. (Executive VP, Chief Credit Officer & Chicago Market President Ahern)

Q&A

  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: Asked for 2Q margin puts-and-takes and whether securities accretion is limited after repositioning; President Harmening said the acquisition margin impact remains what was disclosed and reiterated "a potential increase of 5 to 10 basis points" after marks. (President, CEO & Director Harmening)
  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: Pressed on hiring competitiveness and pricing in new markets; President Harmening described hiring momentum as "a running head start" and said it is "a very different game we're playing right now" versus prior years. (President, CEO & Director Harmening)
  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: Asked how much more funding cost relief is possible without rate cuts; CFO Meyer cited "opportunity for remixing" toward relationship products and said funding "isn't going to stop" potential NII upside. (Executive VP & CFO Meyer)
  • Casey Haire, Autonomous: Asked about loan-yield trends and new-market yields; CFO Meyer said growth is expected to remain concentrated in "C&I and CRE" and that the rate-cut outlook is "very favorable compared to what we thought about at the beginning of the year." (Executive VP & CFO Meyer)
  • Casey Haire, Autonomous: Asked about buybacks and Basel III; President Harmening said he "fully expect[s]" to use the "$100 million authorization" this year, while CFO Meyer said Basel III is "in the comment period" and the outcome is expected "to be favorable for us." (President, CEO & Director Harmening; Executive VP & CFO Meyer)
  • Brandon Rud, Stephens: Asked how much C&I growth was seasonal, including mortgage warehouse; President Harmening said he is "very bullish on C&I growth" and indicated he would place results "at the high end" of the company’s "9% to 10%" C&I growth forecast. (President, CEO & Director Harmening)
  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Asked if stand-alone expenses are drifting higher; President Harmening said the company is "almost flat as a pancake" and "believe[s] the legacy business will manage to that 3% number." (President, CEO & Director Harmening)
  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler: Asked about deposit expectations and mix; President Harmening said deposits are "tracking exactly to where we expected to be" and added, "I would expect the interest-bearing would dominate" deposit growth, with only "modest increase from demand deposits." (President, CEO & Director Harmening)
  • Christopher O’Connell, KBW: Asked about post-close balance sheet positioning and potential runoff; CFO Meyer said unmarked balances came in "right where we expected" and that the company is "materially on track," while President Harmening said there were "no surprises" and "no change" to portfolio views since close. (Executive VP & CFO Meyer; President, CEO & Director Harmening)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral-to-slightly skeptical, centered on acquisition marks, margin path, deposit strategy, expense creep, and capital actions, including repeated framing around "marks" and requests for what changes "if you take American National off" guidance. (Jared Shaw, Barclays; Jon Arfstrom, RBC; Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James)
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more assertive in Q&A, using confident language such as "I'm very bullish" and "I fully expect" on C&I growth and buyback usage, while repeatedly caveating acquisition details with process timing like "we need to get through the marks." (President, CEO & Director Harmening)
  • Versus Q4, management’s messaging shifted from planning the acquisition to executing it, moving from "announced an agreement" to "we announced the closing" and repeatedly stating there were "no major surprises" post-close, while analysts’ focus shifted from approval/all-in deal modeling to near-term margin and purchase accounting impacts. (President, CEO & Director Harmening)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The acquisition narrative advanced from Q4’s "agreement to acquire" to Q1’s "closing" and a defined integration milestone, with conversion still targeted for "late" Q3 and purchase accounting adjustments expected "later this quarter." (President, CEO & Director Harmening)
  • Guidance language on rates and NII tightened: Q4’s NII growth forecast explicitly "assumes 2 Fed rate cuts" and Q1 commentary highlighted fewer cuts in Moody’s outlook and suggested legacy stand-alone NII guidance would be "more like 7% to 8%" under a different rate-cut path. (Executive VP & CFO Meyer; Executive VP, Chief Credit Officer & Chicago Market President Ahern)
  • Capital return posture became more explicit in Q1: Q4 emphasized organic growth as the priority, while Q1 included Harmening’s statement, "I fully expect that we'll use" the "$100 million authorization" this year, alongside continued references to staying within the CET1 range. (President, CEO & Director Harmening)

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged "volatility at the macro level" and credit stressors including "ongoing inflation pressures, shifting labor markets, tariffs and other economic concerns," while maintaining that portfolio performance remained solid and that there had not been "a material shift in the credit profile." (President, CEO & Director Harmening; Executive VP, Chief Credit Officer & Chicago Market President Ahern)
  • Integration execution risk remained a central near-term item, with management repeatedly deferring detailed earnings impacts until purchase accounting is finalized, stating, "We expect to finalize purchase accounting adjustments later this quarter" and that fuller details would come after "we closed the books for second quarter." (President, CEO & Director Harmening; Executive VP & CFO Meyer)

Final Takeaway

Management presented Q1 as a continuation of strong organic momentum, anchored by stated C&I-led loan growth, core customer deposit growth, and stable credit, while shifting investor attention to integration execution after the American National close on April 1. The company emphasized that early post-close work is on schedule, conversion remains targeted for late Q3, and purchase accounting marks are the key gating item for more specific updates, while also layering in updated combined-company 2026 period-end growth expectations and a more explicit intention to use the existing $100 million repurchase authorization this year.

data.title: Ameriprise anticipates Huntington Bank onboarding to add $28B and approximately 260 advisers in Q4
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ameriprise Financial (AMP) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Ameriprise delivered a strong start to the year, driven by our disciplined execution and the benefits of our diversified business,” Jim Cracchiolo said, adding that “ongoing market volatility and economic uncertainty” contributed to “more cautious client behavior.”
  • Cracchiolo highlighted firmwide results including “adjusted operating revenues ... up 11% to $4.8 billion,” “EPS up 19% to a record $11.26,” and ROE that “increased to more than 54%,” alongside “assets under management administration advisement ... 12% to $1.7 trillion.”
  • On Advice & Wealth Management, Cracchiolo said the quarter was “lighter on flows” due to client caution and “some lumpiness in recruiting and terminations,” while noting “$6 billion of wrap net inflows,” “strong transactional activity up 10%,” and “productivity increasing another 10% ... to a record $1.2 million per adviser.”
  • Cracchiolo announced a bank channel win: “we signed a multiyear agreement to become the retail investment program provider [for] the Huntington Bank,” which he said “is expected to add approximately 260 advisers and $28 billion in assets with onboarding beginning later this year.”
  • Walter Berman said results included “adjusted operating earnings per share up 19% to $11.26 and an operating margin of 28%,” and added, “in the quarter, we returned 88% of operating earnings to shareholders through share repurchases and dividends.”

Outlook

  • Management did not provide formal companywide EPS or revenue guidance in this transcript; discussion centered on flow timing, capital return ranges, and business-specific expectations.
  • Berman said Ameriprise “will be buying back 85% to 90%,” and tied pacing to valuation: “certainly looking at the P/E ratio and where we are right now.”
  • On Comerica-related adviser and asset outflows, Berman said, “we anticipate the higher pace of outflows related to Comerica will continue in the second and third quarters culminating with the conversion occurring near the end of the third quarter.” Cracchiolo added, “those flows will continue to come out, and they will be completed at the end of September.”
  • On Retirement & Protection Solutions, Berman said, “we continue to expect earnings over time to be in the $800 million range per year.”

Financial Results

  • Ameriprise reported “adjusted operating revenues ... up 11% to $4.8 billion,” “adjusted operating earnings per share up 19% to $11.26,” and “an operating margin of 28%,” according to Berman.
  • Advice & Wealth Management results included “adjusted operating net revenues increased 14% to $3.2 billion,” pretax adjusted operating earnings “increased 20% to $951 million,” and an item tied to Comerica: “a onetime $25 million make-whole payment.”
  • Asset Management results included operating earnings “increased 13% to $273 million,” revenues “increased 8% to $910 million,” and “margin reached 44% ... above our targeted range of 35% to 39%,” with Berman noting “the back-office transformation is really not taken hold yet.”
  • Retirement & Protection Solutions delivered pretax adjusted operating earnings of “$190 million,” which Berman said reflected “higher distribution expenses associated with strong sales levels” and “continued outflows from variable annuities with living benefits.”

Q&A

  • Wilma Burdis, Raymond James: Why not return more than 88% and what to expect in 2Q? Walter Berman: “we will be buying back 85% to 90%... we certainly have the capacity.”
  • Burdis, Raymond James: Quantify Comerica outflows and discuss Huntington expectations. Berman: outflows to “continue and accelerate ... in the second and third quarter”; Cracchiolo: flows “completed at the end of September,” and Huntington “will come in, in the fourth quarter.”
  • Brennan Hawken, BMO: Is $18 billion Comerica assets and how to phase? Cracchiolo: “it’s hard to know exactly”; Berman: “$18 billion is right.”
  • Hawken, BMO: Cash optimization/AI tools impact? Cracchiolo: sweep is “only a few percent” of earnings and “we’re not as concerned with it”; Berman: “our average balance now is $6,000.”
  • Michael Cyprys, Morgan Stanley: Bank initiatives contribution and ramp? Cracchiolo: pledge is “growing nicely,” checking “just completed the launch,” and “we’re at the early stages.”
  • Cyprys, Morgan Stanley: AI tools today and ability to quantify productivity gains? Cracchiolo: AI is “embedded in an integrated platform,” with eMeeting that “takes away hours of work... every week,” but “we haven’t extrapolated.”
  • Suneet Kamath, Jefferies: Can AWM still hit 4%–5% organic growth amid competition? Berman: “the solid core of our growth is there,” but flows can be “lumpy”; Cracchiolo: strategy is “organic growth, built, not bought.”
  • Kamath, Jefferies: Does AFIG/bank demand for outsourcing WM investment favor AMP? Cracchiolo: “You’re thinking about it 100% correct,” citing Huntington’s selection process.
  • Crispin Love, Piper Sandler: Asset Management margin sustainability and transformation status? Berman: “the back office has not worked its way through the numbers yet.”
  • Love, Piper Sandler: Huntington deal process/competition and timeline? Berman: “the process was over a year.”
  • Steven Chubak, Wolfe: Core NNA expectations and TA stickiness; distribution expense interplay? Berman: “you should see that number pretty much stay in that range,” while noting Comerica noise.
  • Chubak, Wolfe: Signature Wealth adoption/penetration KPIs? Cracchiolo: rollout is “one of the quickest” versus prior wrap launches and the team will “give you some more information.”
  • Thomas Gallagher, Evercore: Scale of aggressive recruiting and risk to adviser base? Cracchiolo: “it isn’t large,” and described “lumpy” impacts from a few practices.
  • Gallagher, Evercore: HBAN deal economics vs smaller recruiting deals? Berman: “It’s a 10-year deal... paybacks... within our ranges,” and “IRRs are very good.”
  • Kenneth Lee, RBC: Timing of $28B AUM transfer? Berman: “We’re targeting that most of that will occur in the fourth quarter.”
  • Lee, RBC: Asset Management G&A outlook? Berman: “should track in being in the range of neutral or a small negative.”
  • Anthony (for Alex Blostein), Goldman Sachs: Where are aggressive recruiting packages and when revise payouts? Berman: “both the W-2 and in the franchise... extremely aggressive.”
  • Anthony (for Blostein), Goldman Sachs: Certificate balance trajectory? Berman: “probably stabilize and stay in this range or increase a little.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts sounded slightly negative to pressing on flow normalization, recruiting competition, and sustainability (e.g., Hawken: “I’d like to get a mark-to-market on Comerica”).
  • Management tone was slightly positive and disciplined, emphasizing profitability and capital return; Cracchiolo framed recruiting as selective and said the Comerica financial impact was “immaterial.”
  • Versus last quarter’s more upbeat backdrop (“overall environment remains quite positive”), Q1 added more caution around volatility, “lumpiness,” and competitive recruiting, while maintaining confidence in the core model.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 described “more cautious client behavior” and “lumpiness in recruiting and terminations,” versus Q4’s “strong organic client flows” and “one of our best quarters for flows.”
  • Capital return shifted from Q4 “over 100% of operating earnings” to Q1 “88%,” while Berman reiterated an “85% to 90%” framework.
  • Asset Management margins were discussed as elevated in both quarters (Q4 “40%” and Q1 “44%”), with Q1 stressing that back-office outsourcing benefits are still ahead.
  • Analyst focus rotated from Q4 flow acceleration and seasonality to Q1’s Comerica attrition, recruiting-price discipline, and the timing/value of the Huntington onboarding.

Risks and Concerns

  • Berman cited “aggressive recruiting environment” driving departures and said current recruiting deals “exceed what we believe is a balanced risk return approach.”
  • Management flagged continued Comerica-related outflows through Q3, with Cracchiolo stating they “will be completed at the end of September.”
  • Asset Management margin sustainability was questioned; Berman said transformation continues and “the back office has not worked its way through the numbers yet.”

Final Takeaway

Ameriprise management emphasized record adjusted EPS of $11.26, 11% adjusted operating revenue growth to $4.8 billion, and ROE above 54% while describing Q1 flow pressure from market volatility, Comerica-related attrition, and a competitive recruiting backdrop. The call also highlighted a multiyear Huntington Bank agreement expected to add approximately 260 advisers and $28 billion in assets, with most onboarding targeted for Q4, alongside continued capital return in an 85%–90% range and ongoing platform investments in Signature Wealth, banking products, and embedded AI.

data.title: KREF outlines plan to cut legacy office exposure to under 10% and authorizes $75M buyback amid dividend reset
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: KKR Real Estate Finance Trust (KREF) Q1 2026

Management View

  • KREF framed 2026 as a portfolio transition and said its focus is "executing an aggressive resolution strategy across our watch list assets and certain legacy office exposures" and "positioning a portion of our REO portfolio for liquidity" (CEO Matthew Salem).
  • Management tied near-term book value pressure to the planned cleanup, saying, "we may choose to incur book value declines as we seek liquidity on legacy assets to create a higher quality portfolio" and added it "see[s] a clear path to redeploy capital in newer vintage, higher quality investments" (CEO Salem).
  • KREF laid out specific 2026 portfolio targets, including, "reduce legacy office exposure from 21% to under 10%" and "resolve all of our current watch list loans by year-end" (CEO Salem).
  • Salem highlighted early execution, stating, "Our largest office loan and $225 million loan in Bellevue was refinanced in the first quarter at par" and added, "the property securing our largest watch list office loan is currently being marketed for sale" (CEO Salem).
  • On capital allocation, KREF announced, "a dividend reduction to $0.10 per share per quarter payable on July 15," while emphasizing, "This decision is not driven by liquidity constraints" (CEO Salem).
  • KREF said buybacks are a key use of capital at current trading levels, noting, "On April 14, our Board authorized a new $75 million share repurchase program" (CEO Salem).
  • Credit actions and reserves were a central theme, with management stating, "we recorded CECL provisions of $74 million, bringing our total allowance to $260 million" (President and COO W. Mattson).
  • REO monetization was described in time buckets, and KREF emphasized the OpenAI lease in Mountain View: "we signed a long-term full property lease with OpenAI" and "expect to bring this asset to market within the next 12 to 16 months" (President and COO Mattson).

Outlook

  • KREF tied the dividend reset to capital allocation and expected earnings volatility, saying the new dividend reflects "a disciplined approach to capital allocation" and is "aligned with our expectations for distributable earnings per share before realized losses" (CEO Salem).
  • Management provided an earnings cadence, stating, "earnings [are] expected to trough in the second half of 2026 into the first half of 2027" and "Once we get through this period, we expect distributable earnings per share to increase" (CEO Salem).
  • KREF positioned available capital for reinvestment, saying it "expect[s] to have over $500 million of capital to invest" and cited "over $2 billion of expected repayments in 2026" as a driver (CEO Salem).

Financial Results

  • For Q1 2026, KREF reported "a GAAP net loss of $62 million or negative $0.96 per share" and said "Book value as of March 31, 2026, is $11.87 per share" (Director of Investor Relations Jack Switala).
  • KREF reported "a distributable loss of $4 million or negative $0.06 per share" and "Distributable earnings before realized losses was $13 million or $0.20 per share" (Director of Investor Relations Switala).
  • The company said it "paid a $0.25 cash dividend in April with respect to the first quarter" (Director of Investor Relations Switala).
  • Liquidity and leverage disclosures included: "we had $653 million of liquidity" including "$135 million of cash on hand" and "$500 million of undrawn capacity on our corporate revolver" (President and COO Mattson).

Q&A

  • William Catherwood, BTIG: Asked whether getting to "50% newer vintage loans by year-end" implied roughly $1.0B-$1.2B of originations; CEO Salem replied, "That's certainly in the ballpark," adding it "will depend a little bit on the share buyback amount."
  • Catherwood, BTIG: Asked whether originations and the $75M buyback would need to be timed to REO sales; CEO Salem said liquidity is expected to come from repayments and added, "No" when asked if REO sales must be lined up to hit the newer-vintage target.
  • Catherwood, BTIG: Asked outcomes for remaining watch list loans; CEO Salem said the goal is "to monetize the vast majority of these" and reiterated, "the goal really is to clear all this up by the end of the year," while noting on two multifamily loans, "we may take small losses or not."
  • Christopher Muller, Citizens JMP: Pressed on whether the $0.10 dividend implies earnings around that level; CEO Salem said, "We think earnings are going to trough towards back half of this year into next year" and noted there "could be a little bit of noise in certain quarters."
  • Muller, Citizens JMP: Asked about a "$42 million" CMBS investment; CEO Salem said KREF is evaluating diversification including "expanding into Europe" and said the CMBS opportunity provided "a little bit duration" and was evaluated on "relative value."
  • Jade Rahmani, KBW: Asked about life science leasing "green shoots"; CEO Salem said, "We are," while adding, "it still feels early" and described Boston as "a little bit behind" South San Francisco.
  • Rahmani, KBW: Questioned risk rating migration and "skipping ahead"; CEO Salem said "the vast majority of cases" follow "3, 4, 5," but added "sometimes there's jump risk around a maturity date or around a modification discussion."
  • Rahmani, KBW: Asked about potential further loss on a marketed Minneapolis office asset; President and COO Mattson said reserve metrics cited were "a blend" and "just a proxy," adding, "we think it's appropriately reserved for."
  • Gabriel Poggi, Raymond James: Asked how buybacks are managed versus leverage; CEO Salem said, "we're not changing our leverage targets" and "we want to kind of stay in that 3.5 to 4x range," while calling the $75M authorization "a lot of firepower."
  • Poggi, Raymond James: Asked about a manager fee cut/waiver; CEO Salem said, "we're evaluating everything" and "all options... are on the table."
  • Poggi, Raymond James: Asked for Mountain View lease details; CEO Salem said, "We're subject to a pretty tight NDA," but described it as "a long-term lease" expected to "trade like a net lease" and stated the asset sale timing is linked to a "stabilized moment."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to skeptical, with focus on dividend sustainability, credit downgrades, reserve adequacy, and risk-rating process, including Rahmani’s comment that "the skipping ahead makes me a little worried about the risk 3 loans" and Poggi’s question on a possible "fee cut, fee waiver."
  • Management tone was slightly positive in strategy framing but acknowledged near-term pressure, including "earnings [are] expected to trough" and that KREF "may choose to incur book value declines" during resolutions (CEO Salem).
  • Versus the prior quarter, management provided more definitive capital actions and timelines (dividend reset, new buyback authorization, and a stated goal to resolve the watch list by year-end), while analysts continued to press on structural valuation discounts and asset-level loss risk.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Reported book value moved to $11.87 at March 31, 2026 from $13.04 at December 31, 2025 (Director of Investor Relations Switala).
  • GAAP net loss was $62 million (-$0.96) in Q1 2026 vs. $32 million (-$0.49) in Q4 2025 (Director of Investor Relations Switala).
  • Dividend strategy shifted from evaluation to action: in Q4 2025 management said the Board was "actively evaluating" the dividend (CEO Salem), while in Q1 2026 KREF announced "a dividend reduction to $0.10" and a new "$75 million share repurchase program" (CEO Salem).
  • REO narrative advanced from tenant engagement in Mountain View (Q4 2025) to a signed lease: "we signed a long-term full property lease with OpenAI" (President and COO Mattson).

Risks and Concerns

  • KREF flagged additional credit stress and reserve actions, including, "we downgraded our Philadelphia office assets" and "downgraded our Boston Life Science asset" and said the Boston life science loan is "expected to transition to REO in the second quarter" with a "realized loss of approximately $37 million" (President and COO Mattson).
  • Management pointed to potential near-term earnings volatility, stating results "may vary in the near term" and that earnings are expected to trough into early 2027 (CEO Salem).
  • On REO execution timing and value realization, management said Mountain View monetization is targeted "within the next 12 to 16 months" and noted a longer hold for certain life science REO "given current market conditions" (President and COO Mattson).

Final Takeaway

KREF described 2026 as a transition year centered on accelerating resolutions across watch list and legacy office exposures, monetizing REO, and rotating into newer-vintage originations, while accepting that book value and earnings could remain pressured during the cleanup. Management paired that strategy with a dividend reset to $0.10 per quarter and a new $75 million buyback authorization, emphasizing liquidity driven by expected repayments and pointing to the OpenAI lease in Mountain View as a key step in improving REO liquidity over the next 12 to 16 months.

data.title: SS&C forecasts 2026 adjusted EPS of $6.74-$7.06 while raising revenue outlook to $6.664B-$6.824B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SS&C Technologies (SSNC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "The first quarter of 2026 included a war in Iran, tariffs war, spiking oil prices and other macro headwinds. Nevertheless, we delivered strong first quarter results, underscoring SS&C's resilience." (Founder, Chairman of the Board & CEO Bill Stone)
  • "Based on our performance and visibility today, we are raising 2026 guidance." (CEO Stone)
  • "First quarter results were adjusted revenue of $1.648 billion, up 9% and adjusted diluted earnings per share of $1.69, a 14% increase." (CEO Stone)
  • "Adjusted organic revenue growth was 5%, with performance driven by GIDS, which grew 10.4%, GlobeOp, which grew 6.7% and our recent acquisitions are executing ahead of expectations." (CEO Stone)
  • "Next week, SS&C will launch Blue Prism WorkHQ, our Agentic workflow orchestration platform designed to coordinate automation, AI agents and human decision-making across enterprise workflows." (CEO Stone)
  • "As a result, adjusted organic revenue growth on a constant currency basis was 5% and our core expenses increased 2.9% or $27 million." (Executive VP & CFO Brian Schell)
  • "We estimate that software, largely in the form of subscriptions, represents 11% of this category." (President & COO Rahul Kanwar)

Outlook

  • Q2 2026 adjusted diluted EPS of $1.64-$1.70 vs. $1.66; Q2 2026 revenue of $1.64B-$1.68B vs. $1.652B (company guidance vs. analysts’ estimates)
  • Full-year 2026 adjusted diluted EPS of $6.74-$7.06; full-year 2026 revenue of $6.664B-$6.824B; and "maintaining our targeted annual EBITDA expansion of 50 basis points with a goal of 40% margin in Q4." (CFO Schell)
  • "Second quarter of '26, we expect revenue to be in the range of $1.64 billion to $1.68 billion and 5.6% organic revenue growth at the midpoint." (CFO Schell)
  • Compared with Q4 2025 guidance language, management shifted from "we expect" full-year 2026 revenue of "$6.654 billion" (low end) to "we increased our expectations" to "$6.664 billion" (low end). (CFO Schell, Q4 2025 vs. Q1 2026)

Financial Results

  • Q1 2026 adjusted revenue of $1.648B vs. $1.630B; Q1 2026 adjusted diluted EPS of $1.69 vs. $1.65 (reported results vs. analysts’ estimates)
  • "Our Q1 2026 GAAP results reflect revenues of $1.647 billion, net income of $226 million and diluted earnings per share of $0.91." (CFO Schell)
  • "Adjusted consolidated EBITDA was a first quarter record of $651 million... and a margin of 39.5%, a 40 basis point expansion." (CFO Schell)
  • "The adjusted revenue increase of $133 million was primarily driven by incremental revenue contributions from GIDS of $38 million, GlobeOp of $29 million and a favorable impact from foreign exchange of $22 million." (CFO Schell)
  • "For the 3 months ended March 31, 2026, cash from operating activities was $300 million, up 10% year-over-year." (CEO Stone)
  • "SS&C ended the first quarter with $421 million in cash and cash equivalents and $7.5 billion in gross debt... Resulting net leverage ratio was 2.76x." (CFO Schell)

Q&A

  • Kevin McVeigh, UBS: asked whether macro conditions held back results; CEO Stone: "you get hesitancy... when you have tariffs... And then you have war and then you have spiking oil prices" but "we're pretty bullish on 2026."
  • McVeigh, UBS: asked about AUA growth drivers; CEO Stone: "we grew AUA $581 billion since the first quarter of 2024" and tied drivers to "market appreciation" and "increasing allocations" to global macro hedge funds.
  • Daniel Perlin, RBC: asked about private credit redemptions risk; COO Kanwar: "the vast majority of our private credit funds are closed-end fund structures" and "we're pretty immune from day-to-day fluctuations."
  • Perlin, RBC: asked about GIDS cadence and Australia; CEO Stone: "the superannuation market in Australia is [ $4 trillion ]" and "I would guess that GIDS is going to do very well in 2026."
  • Jeffrey Schmitt, William Blair: asked where AI creates risk; CEO Stone: "we don't really look at AI as a threat" and emphasized regulatory complexity and constant rule changes.
  • Schmitt, William Blair: asked about buyback pace; CEO Stone: "yes, we're quite bullish" and referenced quarter cash uses (bonuses and taxes).
  • Surinder Thind, Jefferies: asked if Blue Prism could drive material growth inflection; CEO Stone: "AI is not just a gas pedal. Somebody better have a break" and argued SS&C’s controls and domain expertise limit disruption.
  • Thind, Jefferies: asked about investment levels vs. margin targets; CEO Stone: "there's a lot of opportunities for us to drive margin" but questioned taking money from "R&D or other initiatives" despite "flexibility."
  • Peter Heckmann, D.A. Davidson: asked about tokenization preparedness; COO Kanwar: "we have customers that are tokenized today" and said onboarding can be simpler while "the rest of the work stays exactly the same"; CEO Stone added Calastone: "Which we spent $1 billion acquiring."
  • Heckmann, D.A. Davidson: asked if Calastone outperformed; CFO Schell: "They continue to perform well. And so that was a strong quarter by them."
  • Bala Kumaj (for Alexei), JPMorgan: asked if Intralinks improvement is market vs. share gains; COO Kanwar: "the market has come back a little" and product investments and "AI-enabled modules" have helped "gain some market share."
  • Bala Kumaj (for Alexei), JPMorgan: asked about health care sustainability; CEO Stone: cited market size and said "the government is, I think, going to use Humana... to administer that program" and "we think that we could be a winner."
  • James Faucette, Morgan Stanley: asked about wealth drivers and Q2 risks; CEO Stone: said Black Diamond and Trust Suite are benefiting as assets move "through trust" and said Black Diamond "is going to continue to grow in excess of double digits."
  • Faucette, Morgan Stanley: asked about AI monetization vs. internal productivity; CEO Stone: "we have deployed close to 4,000 digital workers" and said it "maybe saved us a couple of hundred million dollars a year"; COO Kanwar: "We are seeing a positive impact" in software development speed.
  • Patrick O'Shaughnessy, Raymond James: asked about blockchain disintermediation in GIDS; COO Kanwar: "I think it's mostly an opportunity" and said tokenization examples are "still fairly small" while simplifying some work.
  • O'Shaughnessy, Raymond James: asked why GlobeOp growth slowed; CEO Stone: "it just depends timing-wise" on clients going live and renewal timing.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive, including "exceptional results" and "terrific part to the story" (Kevin McVeigh, UBS), with skeptical probes focused on AI risk, private credit redemptions, buyback pace, and blockchain/tokenization.
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and opportunity-focused, while acknowledging macro pressure; CEO Stone cited "a lot of macro headwinds" but said "we're pretty bullish on 2026" and framed AI governance with "AI is not just a gas pedal. Somebody better have a break."
  • Versus Q4 2025, prepared remarks sounded less defensive about the "sell-off in software company shares" and more centered on raising guidance and launching WorkHQ, while Q&A retained control-and-regulation messaging in both quarters (CEO Stone: "we don't really look at AI as a threat" vs. Q4’s "Large language models sometimes have hallucinations").

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance moved higher in Q1 2026: "we are raising 2026 guidance" (CEO Stone) and CFO Schell said "we increased our expectations" for full-year revenue and adjusted EPS ranges versus the Q4 2025 outlook.
  • Strategic emphasis broadened from Q4’s AI-competitor backdrop to Q1’s product launch cadence: "SS&C will launch Blue Prism WorkHQ" and "registration right now is over 2,000 people." (CEO Stone)
  • Analysts expanded topic breadth in Q1 (private credit, tokenization, blockchain, wealth cadence, AI ROI) versus Q4’s heavier concentration on health care weakness and AI/automation decision delays.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Stone highlighted macro uncertainty: "tariffs" and "war" and "spiking oil prices" creating "hesitancy." (CEO Stone)
  • AI governance and regulatory expectations remained a recurring risk frame: "AI is not just a gas pedal..." and regulators can change requirements "whenever they want." (CEO Stone)
  • Private credit redemption fears were addressed as manageable: "the vast majority... are closed-end fund structures" and "we're pretty immune from day-to-day fluctuations." (COO Kanwar)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a record quarter with raised 2026 guidance, framed AI as a structural tailwind supported by Blue Prism (including the imminent WorkHQ launch), and emphasized embedded, regulated workflows as protection against disruption. Executives also pointed to continued capital return priorities, with CEO Stone saying share repurchase conviction has strengthened and repurchases are prioritized absent "high-quality accretive acquisitions," while analysts pressed on AI risk, tokenization/blockchain, private credit, and segment growth cadence.

data.title: Carlisle reaffirms 2026 outlook of low single-digit revenue growth and ~50 bps margin expansion while targeting $1b buybacks
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Carlisle Companies Incorporated (CSL) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Revenue for the first quarter was $1.1 billion, down 4% year-over-year" and the decline was attributed to winter weather delays and "approximately $15 million of tariff-related order pull forward from Canadian customers, which did not repeat this year" (Chairman, President & CEO D. Koch).
  • "Adjusted EPS rising to $3.63, up 1% versus last year" and "adjusted EBITDA margin expanding by 50 basis points to 22.3%" were presented as outcomes of multi-quarter productivity and cost actions under COS despite pressured volumes (Chairman, President & CEO Koch).
  • "We acted quickly in mid-March announcing price increases across both CCM and CWT effective mid-April and implementing real-time freight surcharges" and "announced a second round of price increases at CCM today" amid oil-driven input and freight volatility (Chairman, President & CEO Koch).
  • "We are reaffirming our full year 2026 outlook of low single-digit revenue growth and approximately 50 basis points of adjusted EBITDA margin expansion" (Chairman, President & CEO Koch).
  • "Adjusted EBITDA was $235 million in the quarter" and the margin improvement was tied to "COS-driven productivity gains, procurement discipline and efficient management of selling and administrative costs" (VP & CFO Kevin Zdimal).

Outlook

  • "We are reaffirming our 2026 outlook" with "full year consolidated revenue growth in the low single-digit range" and "consolidated adjusted EBITDA margins to expand by approximately 50 basis points for the full year" (VP & CFO Zdimal).
  • Management shifted its revenue-growth positioning within the range: "with our recent price increase announcements, we now expect revenue growth at the higher end of that range, along with double-digit growth for EPS" (VP & CFO Zdimal).
  • The pricing-led mix of the higher revenue assumption was described as: moving from "probably 1%" to "3%" and "all of that improvement is price" (VP & CFO Zdimal).

Financial Results

  • Consolidated results included "first quarter revenue of $1.1 billion" and "adjusted EBITDA margin of 22.3%" with "adjusted EPS ... $3.63" (VP & CFO Zdimal).
  • CCM delivered "revenue of $758 million" and "adjusted EBITDA margin increased 30 basis points to 27.4%" (VP & CFO Zdimal).
  • CWT delivered "Q1 revenue of $294 million" and "adjusted EBITDA margin was 15.2%, a decrease of 40 basis points" (VP & CFO Zdimal).
  • Liquidity and leverage were cited as: "$771 million in cash and cash equivalents" plus "$1 billion available under our revolving credit facility" and "net debt-to-EBITDA ratio was 1.7x" (VP & CFO Zdimal).
  • Cash flow and capital allocation details included "net cash used in operating activities was $45 million" and "free cash flow used in continuing operations was $73 million," reflecting "a $125 million post year-end settlement of an accrued tax-related liability"; shareholder returns were "$250 million of share repurchases" and "$46 million of dividends" while "maintaining our pace" toward "our annual repurchase target for 2026 of $1 billion" (VP & CFO Zdimal).

Q&A

  • Susan Maklari, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc.: Asked about new products and price elasticity; Chairman, President & CEO Koch said Carlisle plans "just over 10, 12 new products this year," highlighted "ThermaThin R7 insulation" and noted it "won 2 awards" at IRE, but "won't really hit the mark in terms of deliveries till, probably July" and is "not really impacting any growth in Q1 or Q2."
  • Maklari, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc.: Asked on CWT margin improvement; Chairman, President & CEO Koch said the team had a "goal ... of getting as close as we could to 20%" and cited "automation," "footprint consolidation" and "in-sourcing," while VP & CFO Zdimal added quarterly cadence expectations: "Q2 might be around 19%" and "improving to 22% in Q3" and "at least 100 basis points of margin improvement" for full-year CWT.
  • Timothy Wojs, Baird: Pressed on pricing stickiness and supply-shock dynamics; Chairman, President & CEO Koch said "the stickiness with the price increases is going to be pretty good" given visibility into oil and freight and noted job protections for existing quotes; VP & CFO Zdimal provided CCM margin cadence: "approaching ... 31%" in Q2, "slightly exceed ... 31%" in Q3, and "right around 28%" in Q4.
  • Bryan Blair, Oppenheimer: Asked volume vs. price in the guide; VP & CFO Zdimal said the move within the range was "all ... price" with "second half of the year" seeing more realization; Chairman, President & CEO Koch added price/raws was forecast to "hold it neutral."
  • Tomohiko Sano, JPMorgan: Asked about distributor inventory normalization and consolidation; Chairman, President & CEO Koch said inventories were "moving into ... a more normal inventory situation" and cited ongoing improvement with QXO: "they continue to improve" and consolidation like TopBuild was "not as impactful to Carlisle" due to limited overlap.
  • Ryan Merkel, William Blair: Asked about Q2 revenue seasonality and weather; Chairman, President & CEO Koch said weather impact was "about 3 days" and "around $30 million, $35 million" and said trends improved as weather improved; VP & CFO Zdimal detailed seasonal revenue splits for CCM and CWT.
  • David MacGregor, Longbow: Asked about elasticity, deferrals, and warranty; Chairman, President & CEO Koch acknowledged "some project delays" but said it "hasn't been as impactful," and stated "warranty is still a driver" for larger projects.
  • Adam Baumgarten, Vertical Research: Asked magnitude of price increases and price/cost; VP & CFO Zdimal said full-year price/cost assumption was "neutral" and Chairman, President & CEO Koch said the second CCM increase was "approximately 5% to 8%."
  • McClaran Hayes, Zelman: Asked input inflation embedded in guidance; VP of Investor Relations Mehul Patel said neutral price/cost implies "about high single-digit raw material inflation" as a percent of raws.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly negative to pressing, centered on pricing stickiness, distributor inventories, elasticity/project deferrals, and raw-material inflation assumptions, including questions on "order volatility" and whether costs could drive deferrals.
  • Management sentiment was slightly positive but cautious, emphasizing execution and pricing actions while flagging uncertainty: "we remain cautious about the second half given the ongoing geopolitical volatility" and "we don't know how much that could impact demand or not" (Chairman, President & CEO Koch; VP & CFO Zdimal).
  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s tone moved from expecting some raw-material tailwind (Q4) to heightened focus on oil-linked volatility and neutrality in price/cost, while reiterating long-term targets: "We remain confident in Vision 2030 and our long-term financial targets of $40 of adjusted EPS and 25% plus ROIC" (VP & CFO Zdimal).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The core framework stayed consistent: in Q4 the company guided "low single-digit revenue growth and approximately 50 basis points of adjusted EBITDA margin expansion" for 2026, and in Q1 it again said it was "reaffirming" that outlook (Chairman, President & CEO Koch).
  • Q1 introduced a more explicit pricing response to geopolitics and oil volatility (two rounds of price increases plus freight surcharges), while Q4 commentary expected pricing largely "flat" in key categories and referenced some raw-material relief beginning in Q2.
  • Analyst focus broadened from Vision 2030 levers and baseline pricing visibility (Q4) to near-term execution mechanics: timing of price realization, distribution-channel inventory normalization, and sensitivity of projects to cost shocks (Q1).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Recent geopolitical escalation has materially increased uncertainty in global energy markets" with risks tied to "the Iran conflict" and potential disruption through "the Straits of Hormuz" (Chairman, President & CEO Koch).
  • New construction softness persisted: "New construction remains soft across both residential and nonresidential markets" and the outlook "does not assume a near-term recovery" in a "higher for longer" rate environment (Chairman, President & CEO Koch).
  • Input-cost inflation and availability risks were highlighted, with management prepared to "take additional pricing actions" if "structural cost levels reset higher" and monitoring supply-chain disruption in petrochemicals (Chairman, President & CEO Koch).

Final Takeaway

Carlisle’s management described a Q1 shaped by weather and a lapped tariff pull-forward, but emphasized margin expansion, reaffirmed 2026 targets for low single-digit revenue growth and about 50 basis points of adjusted EBITDA margin expansion, and repositioned expected growth toward the higher end of the range as pricing actions and freight surcharges flow through. The most prominent near-term swing factors were geopolitically driven petrochemical and freight costs, the timing and stickiness of successive CCM and broader segment price increases, and whether new-construction weakness and project deferrals intensify even as reroofing activity remains a stabilizing base.

data.title: SES AI reaffirms $30M-$35M 2026 revenue outlook as it adds a $20M North America ESS distribution deal
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SES AI Corporation (SES) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "The first quarter revenue came in at $6.7 million, a 47% increase over the fourth quarter" (Founder, Chairman & CEO Qichao Hu), and "We are reaffirming our full-year 2026 revenue guidance of $30 million to $35 million, with contributions expected from all 3 of our revenue-generating business units" (CEO Hu).
  • "Jing will be transitioning from her role as Chief Financial Officer effective April 27" (CEO Hu), and "We have appointed Ray Liu as our new CFO effective April 27" (CEO Hu).
  • "We have now entered the North American market through our multi-year distribution agreement with ATG EPower" (CEO Hu), and "This contract valued at approximately $20 million over 3 years" (CEO Hu).
  • "We have completed the conversion of our manufacturing line at our Chungju, South Korea facility from EV pouch cells to drone-format pouch cells" (CEO Hu), and "Early this month, we began shipping NDAA-compliant cells produced in our Chungju factory to prospective defense and commercial drone customers for evaluation and qualification testing" (CEO Hu).
  • "During the quarter, a major global battery manufacturer committed to a multi-year subscription of our Molecular Universe Search-in-a-Box product" (CEO Hu), while "the direct on-premise revenue from the Molecular Universe continues to build and is expected to make a modest direct contribution in 2026" (CEO Hu).
  • "We utilized approximately $20 million in cash for operations during the first quarter" (Chief Financial Officer Jing Nealis), and "We exited the first quarter with a strong liquidity position of approximately $178 million" (CFO Nealis).

Outlook

  • "We are reaffirming our full-year 2026 revenue guidance of $30 million to $35 million" (CEO Hu).
  • "We remain on track to deliver approximately 15% reduction in full-year operating expenses that we guided on our last call" (CFO Nealis).
  • "We expect revenue in Q2 for the NDAA-compliant cells and then started to pick up in Q3 and in Q4 and then really next year 2027 is going to be a full-year" (CEO Hu).
  • Compared with the prior call’s segment commentary, management shifted from quantifying ESS within guidance ("probably around 65% will come from ESS at least" (CFO Nealis, Q4 2025)) to a more qualitative mix view ("this year to come predominantly from ESS and then rest split between drones and materials" (CEO Hu, Q1 2026)).

Financial Results

  • Q1 revenue was $6.7 million vs. $3,651,000.0 (analysts’ revenue estimate for fiscal quarter 1, fiscal year 2026).
  • GAAP net loss was $12.1 million, or (-$0.04) per share (CFO Nealis), while non-GAAP net loss was $11.1 million, or (-$0.03) per share vs. (-$0.03) (analysts’ EPS estimate for fiscal quarter 1, fiscal year 2026).
  • "As a reminder, the fourth quarter of 2025 was impacted by approximately $1.5 million of revenue that was pushed into the first quarter" (CFO Nealis).
  • "Our Q1 gross margin on a GAAP basis was 18.1%, compared to 11.3% in the fourth quarter of 2025" (CFO Nealis), and "Adjusted EBITDA for the first quarter of 2026 was a loss of $12.8 million, compared to a loss of $13.8 million in the fourth quarter of 2025" (CFO Nealis).
  • "In Q1 2026, we recorded a $4.2 million non-cash gain related to these liabilities" (CFO Nealis), referring to sponsor earn-out mark-to-market movements.
  • "We expect to file a new S-3 shelf registration statement concurrent with our 10-Q, as our current shelf expires on April 28" (CFO Nealis), adding, "This is a routine administrative filing to maintain our financial flexibility" (CFO Nealis).

Q&A

  • Derek Soderberg, Cantor Fitzgerald & Co.: asked drone qualification timing; CEO Hu replied, "Qualification typically 1 to 2 quarters" and said the current phase includes "supply-chain audit." Soderberg also asked to quantify on-premise contributions; CEO Hu responded, "Probably in the next quarter." Soderberg asked defense vs. commercial interest; CEO Hu said, "It's mostly defense" and "we don't have a specific breakdown."
  • Yan Dong, Deutsche Bank: asked if ATG EPower is wholesale and shipment cadence; CEO Hu said, "it's a wholesale distribution" and CFO Nealis added revenue recognition is "based on shipments" and "once we ship it" depending on Incoterms. Dong asked ESS seasonality; CFO Nealis said "Q2, Q3 usually are higher than Q4" but "we see growth quarter-over-quarter." Dong asked guidance contribution by segment; CEO Hu said revenue should come "predominantly from ESS."
  • David Joseph Storms, Stonegate: asked how Edgebox helps the sales cycle; CEO Hu said industry error can be "7% or even as high as 10%" and Edgebox can reduce it to "3% or even less," enabling customers to "buy less" capacity and bid into trading with better estimation. Storms asked materials timing; CEO Hu described testing as "6 to 9 months" plus "another quarter for the commercial qualification." Storms asked expense-reduction cadence; CFO Nealis said "full quarter impact starting from Q3" with "a little bit of a reduction in Q2."
  • Sean Milligan, Needham: asked revenue implications of 1 million drone cells; CEO Hu cited a market price range of "$25 to $35" per NDAA-compliant cell, implying "about $25 million to $35 million" for 1 million units, and said demand could scale "much higher." Milligan asked pipeline sizing; CEO Hu said "a few dozen customers" and reiterated Q2 revenue expectations with ramping in Q3 and Q4.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical and detail-seeking, pressing for cadence and quantification ("what kind of shipment cadence" (Yan Dong, Deutsche Bank) and "Is there any chance you can quantify" (Derek Soderberg, Cantor Fitzgerald & Co.)).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks ("We had a strong start for 2026" (CEO Hu)) and more measured in Q&A when pressed on disclosure ("Probably in the next quarter" (CEO Hu)) and mix breakdown ("predominantly from ESS" (CEO Hu)).
  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s emphasis stayed on execution but moved from EV slowdowns ("the EV market is slowing down" (CEO Hu, Q4 2025)) toward commercialization mechanics in ESS distribution and drone supply-chain audits ("customers come in and do the supply-chain audit" (CEO Hu, Q1 2026)).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management reiterated the same 2026 revenue range in both calls ("$30 million to $35 million" (CFO Nealis, Q4 2025) and "$30 million to $35 million" (CEO Hu, Q1 2026)).
  • The current call added leadership change specificity ("transitioning... effective April 27" and "appointed Ray Liu" (CEO Hu)), not present in the prior call.
  • ESS expansion language progressed from early traction ("sign a multiyear $20 million contract" (CEO Hu, Q4 2025)) to framing it as North America entry and describing it as a wholesale distribution model ("entered the North American market" and "wholesale distribution" (CEO Hu, Q1 2026)).
  • Analyst focus shifted from EV roadmap and OEM relationships in Q4 ("when are you going to commercialize that for EVs?" (Derek Soderberg, Cantor Fitzgerald & Co., Q4 2025)) to near-term conversion metrics, qualification timelines, and revenue cadence in Q1 (drone audits, Edgebox benefits, and shipment-based revenue recognition).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Qualification typically 1 to 2 quarters" (CEO Hu) and dependent supply-chain audits highlighted timing risk for converting evaluations into orders.
  • "The fourth quarter of 2025 was impacted by approximately $1.5 million of revenue that was pushed into the first quarter" (CFO Nealis), flagging shipment timing sensitivity.
  • "Our GAAP net loss in any given quarter can be meaningfully impacted" by earn-out fair value changes (CFO Nealis), including "a $4.2 million non-cash gain" (CFO Nealis).

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as an execution-focused quarter anchored by $6.7 million in revenue, reaffirmed full-year 2026 revenue guidance of $30 million to $35 million, and highlighted catalysts tied to an approximately $20 million, three-year North American ESS distribution agreement and progressing NDAA-compliant drone-cell qualification. The company also underscored cost-reduction pacing into Q3, a CFO transition effective April 27, and liquidity of approximately $178 million as it plans to scale ESS distribution, convert drone evaluations into revenue starting in Q2, and continue building Molecular Universe subscriptions and materials testing momentum.

data.title: Heritage Financial projects noninterest expense of $56M-$57M in Q4 as Olympic integration continues
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Heritage Financial (HFWA) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "We closed the merger with Olympic Bancorp during the first quarter, better positioning our company for growth in the Puget Sound market." (CEO, President & Director Bryan McDonald)
  • "We are converting systems in late September and we will be carrying higher expenses until after the conversion." (CEO, President & Director McDonald)
  • "We expect the upward trajectory to continue, primarily driven by new loans and repricing within the existing loan portfolio." (CEO, President & Director McDonald)
  • "Total loan balances increased $939 million in the first quarter" and "Total deposits increased $1.33 billion in Q1." (Executive VP & CFO Donald Hinson)
  • "The net interest margin increased to 3.96% from 3.72% in the prior quarter" and "We recognized a reversal of provision for credit losses in the amount of $1.03 million in Q1." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • "We are expecting quarterly noninterest expense levels to increase to an average of approximately $64 million to $65 million in Q2 and Q3 before decreasing to a range of $56 million to $57 million in Q4." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • "Nonaccrual loans totaled $15 million at quarter end" and "there were no nonaccrual loans in the acquired Olympic portfolio at quarter end." (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Tony Chalfant)
  • "Our ratio of total nonowner-occupied CRE loans to total loans moved just above the regulatory guidance level to 301%." (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Chalfant)
  • "For the quarter, our commercial team closed $166 million in new loan commitments" and "Based on the current pipeline, we expect an annualized loan growth rate in the mid-single-digit range in the next couple of quarters." (CEO, President & Director McDonald)

Outlook

  • "Based on the current pipeline, we expect an annualized loan growth rate in the mid-single-digit range in the next couple of quarters." (CEO, President & Director McDonald)
  • "Yes. I think we're going to continue to see margin expansion, not going to be significant" and "I expect to reach the 4% by the end of the year or before." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • "I think we're going to stay right around that for the remainder of the year, hovering around 1.70%." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s discussion of integration timing, management reiterated the conversion timing as "late September" and maintained that expenses stay elevated until after conversion. (CEO, President & Director McDonald; Executive VP & CFO Hinson)

Financial results

  • "Q1 yields in the loan portfolio were 5.73%" and "The cost of interest-bearing deposits decreased to 1.71% from 1.83% in the prior quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • "The net interest margin increased to 3.96% from 3.72% in the prior quarter and from 3.44% in the first quarter of 2025." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • "We recognized a reversal of provision for credit losses in the amount of $1.03 million in Q1" and management tied it to "adjusting the allowance from 1.10% at the end of 2025 to 1.06% at the end of Q1." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • "The increase in the noninterest expense was also due to merger-related costs of $5.2 million" and "intangible asset amortization expense of $2.1 million" (each compared by management to $385,000 and $285,000, respectively, in the prior quarter). (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • "Our TCE ratio was 9.6% at the end of Q1 compared to 10.1% in the prior quarter" and "The decrease in the TCE ratio was expected due to the impact of the merger." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)

Q&A

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Expenses looked "kind of high" and asked whether the $64 million to $65 million outlook includes merger costs; Executive VP & CFO Hinson: "yes -- yes, that does include the merger-related expenses" and "If you take out merger costs, we're more in the $57 million to $58 million range" with "dropping to about $55 million by Q4."
  • Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked where the $5 million-plus merger costs sit by line item; Executive VP & CFO Hinson: "professional services would be a big one" plus "compensation because of severance" and "some contract stuff" that could show in "data processing," adding "I don't have it broken out by type."
  • Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked about margin impacts and March run-rate; Executive VP & CFO Hinson: "On the interest reversals, it was 5" (basis points) to margin, and "if you take out the interest reversals for March NIM, it was 3.95%."
  • Jackson Laurent, Stephens: Asked what changed loan-growth expectations and early read on Kitsap bankers; CEO, President & Director McDonald: "The driver behind the go-forward growth rate is really the change in the pipeline" and "we feel good about that pipeline driving kind of mid-single-digit loan growth"; Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Chalfant: "the credit cultures were pretty similar" and "we're feeling pretty comfortable."
  • Laurent, Stephens: Pressed on deposit repricing risk; Executive VP & CFO Hinson: "We did raise our very highest rate" and "I really don't expect it to move a whole lot"; CEO, President & Director McDonald: "we are seeing strong deposit competition" while citing "good success with our relationship strategy."
  • Laurent, Stephens: Asked about buybacks/loss trades; Executive VP & CFO Hinson: "We don't foresee at this point any loss trades" and "We still have about 800,000 shares left in our current repurchase plan" and "we may be active this quarter."
  • Charles Driscoll, KBW: Asked about recruiting amid "disruption" and whether hiring is embedded in expense run-rate; CEO, President & Director McDonald: "We are out recruiting" but "we're not seeing necessarily an increase in total banker headcount" and hiring has been "onesie, twosie" with retirements.
  • Driscoll, KBW: Asked whether integration is ahead/behind schedule and retention/synergies; CEO, President & Director McDonald: "we're right on track" and "there hasn't been any kind of negative customer response" so far, adding "we'll learn more... when we actually go through the systems conversion."
  • Evan Kwiatkowski, Raymond James: Asked about borrower sentiment/payoffs amid macro uncertainty; CEO, President & Director McDonald: "we're not seeing" customers delaying yet, and "that payoff activity is lower than what we encountered last year" with the trend expected "something lower than last year."
  • Kwiatkowski, Raymond James: Asked where pressures are building in credit; Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Chalfant: "we have seen a little more pressure in the C&I portfolio" tied to "uncertainty in the economy, whether it's tariff issues, higher labor costs, supply chain issues."

Sentiment analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, concentrated on cost control and deposit competition, with questions like "it seems kind of high" on expenses (Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson) and whether competition could drive "upward migration" in deposit costs (Jackson Laurent, Stephens).
  • Neutral-to-slightly positive management tone, emphasizing integration progress and stability, including "we're right on track" (CEO, President & Director McDonald) and "credit quality remained strong and stable" (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Chalfant), while also acknowledging uncertainty with "we are aware of the emerging risks in the economy" (Chief Credit Officer Chalfant).
  • Versus the prior quarter’s tone, management shifted from anticipating the Olympic closing to operating as a combined company and discussing concrete post-close items such as "higher expenses until after the conversion" and explicit quarterly expense ranges. (CEO, President & Director McDonald; Executive VP & CFO Hinson)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The major narrative change was execution: Q4 framed Olympic as "pending" with a plan to "close at the end of January" (CEO, President & Director McDonald), while Q1 reported "We closed the merger with Olympic Bancorp during the first quarter" and outlined the "late September" conversion timeline and associated expense drag. (CEO, President & Director McDonald)
  • Guidance language around profitability leaned more specific in Q1, with Executive VP & CFO Hinson setting quarterly noninterest expense ranges and saying "I expect to reach the 4% by the end of the year or before" on net interest margin, compared with Q4’s broader view that margin improvement would "continue over the next year or 2" and could "potentially get near that 4% range by the end of the year." (Executive VP & CFO Hinson)
  • Analyst focus remained consistent on margin and expenses (Jeff Rulis in both quarters), but Q1 added more integration-execution questions (customer/producer retention, synergy timing) and recruiting opportunity questions tied to regional disruption. (Charles Driscoll, KBW)

Risks and concerns

  • "We are converting systems in late September and we will be carrying higher expenses until after the conversion." (CEO, President & Director McDonald)
  • "As of quarter end, our ratio of total nonowner-occupied CRE loans to total loans moved just above the regulatory guidance level to 301%" and management said, "we anticipate the ratio will decline to historical levels over time." (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Chalfant)
  • "We are aware of the emerging risks in the economy and the potential impact on our credit quality" and cited pressure factors including "tariff issues, higher labor costs, supply chain issues." (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Chalfant)
  • Management acknowledged market funding pressure with "strong deposit competition" while emphasizing a relationship strategy to manage mix and costs. (CEO, President & Director McDonald)

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as the first quarter operating with Olympic onboard, with emphasis on a late-September systems conversion that keeps expenses elevated into Q3, an expectation that quarterly noninterest expense moderates in Q4, and a continued upward net interest margin trajectory supported by asset repricing and Olympic’s balance sheet. Credit commentary centered on stable-to-improving nonaccrual metrics, heightened attention to emerging C&I pressures tied to macro uncertainty, and monitoring a nonowner-occupied CRE concentration that moved just above regulatory guidance due to the acquisition and fair value marks.
